macro_segments.title: The case of knowing the field is stolen (Hikir bah she'eina shelo) and the debate between Rav and Shmuel

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the case: Michael knows the field is stolen but purchases it anyway
Rav's position: Michael can recover principal but not improvements (shevach)
Shmuel's position: Michael cannot recover even the principal
The basis of their debate: Whether the transaction is a pikadon (deposit) or matana (gift)
Rav's interpretation: Michael gave money as a deposit to hold, knowing no legal transfer occurred
Shmuel's interpretation: Michael gave the money as a gift

macro_segments.title: Parallel case of marriage to sister (mekadesh achoto) and comparison with field case

micro_segments.title: The case of betrothal to one's sister with money whose status is disputed
Rava explains why both cases must be taught separately despite parallel logic
In field case, stranger unlikely to receive gift; in sister case, relative more likely to

macro_segments.title: The practical problem: How does Michael use the field if it's merely a deposit or gift?

micro_segments.title: Gemara raises the question: Why would Michael enter the field and harvest if no transaction occurred?
Gemara's resolution: Michael assumes he can use it the same way the seller was using it, independent of money
The money's status depends on Rav or Shmuel only after owner reclaims the field

macro_segments.title: Halacha rulings protecting the purchaser against Shmuel's position

micro_segments.title: First ruling: Purchaser recovers principal and shevach even if field turns out stolen (mekach taaut)
Second ruling: Purchaser recovers even if knowing field was stolen, and gets money back
Third ruling: Sofer's omission of lien is an oversight; lien presumed in all sale documents
Summary: Comprehensive protection for purchaser against Shmuel across all scenarios

macro_segments.title: Can the seller later legitimize a bogus sale by buying from the original owner? (Chazar ve-lakach)

micro_segments.title: The case: Seller buys field from original owner after selling it to Michael—does this help Michael?
Rav's answer: Seller's original sale transferred all future rights (kol zechus she-tavo le-yado)
Mar Zutra's reasoning: Seller doesn't want to be called a ganav (thief)
Rav Ashi's reasoning: Seller wants to be seen as trustworthy (heimanuteih)

macro_segments.title: Distinguishing cases where seller's purchase from original owner does and does not validate earlier sale

micro_segments.title: If seller sells to multiple people or gives gift after Michael, no validation of Michael's sale
If seller inherits field from original owner later, field reverts to seller (no validation)
If seller collects debt from original owner using this field, validation depends on seller's selectivity
If seller chose this field specifically over other available fields, validates Michael's sale
If seller took this field only because it was the only asset available, no validation

macro_segments.title: If original owner gives field to seller as gift, does this validate sale to Michael?

micro_segments.title: Debate: Is a gift received like an inheritance (accidental, no validation) or like a purchase (effortful, validates)?
First view: Gift is like inheritance—comes by chance, doesn't validate Michael's sale
Second view: Gift is like sale—requires seller's effort to obtain, so validates Michael's sale

macro_segments.title: Time limits on when seller's acquisition validates the original sale to Michael

micro_segments.title: Rav Huna: Validation continues until case reaches court (amada b'din)
Once field is returned to original owner and creditors begin collection process, validation no longer applies
Rav Papa: Validation ends when auction announcements for seller's property begin

macro_segments.title: How does Michael acquire field if sale to him was originally void? (Gemara's mechanism question)

micro_segments.title: Rami bar Chama challenges: Original sale document is worthless; how does Michael gain title later?
Rava's answer: Michael's trust in seller (samcha da'ateih) creates gemirut da'at sufficient without formal kinyan
Irony: Michael trusts seller precisely because both know field is stolen

macro_segments.title: Rav Sheshes challenges: Can you sell futures that you don't yet own or control?

micro_segments.title: Case of selling what you will inherit from dying father or fish you will catch—does sale work?

macro_segments.title: Rav Sheshes challenges: Can you sell futures that you don't yet own or control? (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rabi Abba bar Zavda: Question is too obvious to discuss (one view) or needs investigation (Rava's view)
Distinction: Inheritance today works due to urgency (funeral expenses); fish today works as urgent need

macro_segments.title: Rav's rule on selling futures and Rambam's market-readiness distinction

micro_segments.title: Rav rules: Can sell a future field (unspecified field, readily available on market)
Rava refines: Only works if field is unspecified (stam); specific field (zu) doesn't work
Gemara reveals: Rav actually holds like Rabbi Meir—can sell any future, even specific things
Rabbi Meir's proof: Betrothal conditional on future events (husband's death, etc.) works despite uncertainty
Rambam's synthesis: Davar shelo ba le'olam works if readily available in market; futures doctrine applies

topical_tags.term: Hikir bah she'eina shelo (knowing field is stolen)
Kol zechus she-tavo le-yado (all future rights)
Chazar ve-lakach (bought back from original owner)
Davar shelo ba le'olam (thing not yet in existence)
Rabbi Meir vs. Chachamim (on selling futures)
